name: Dynamic Array

x.invariant: capacity ≥ length; live elements occupy one contiguous block over indices [0, length).
x.memoryOverhead: Up to ~2× the live element bytes between growth events (spare capacity), plus a pointer + length + capacity.
x.locality: Excellent — fully contiguous; ideal for sequential scans, hardware prefetching and SIMD.
x.mutationModel: In-place mutable; reallocation on growth invalidates pointers, references and iterators.
x.concurrency: Not thread-safe; concurrent push/read race on the buffer pointer and length. Guard externally or use a concurrent vector.

def: A contiguous, automatically-resizing sequence with amortised O(1) append.
problem: A growable list with cache-friendly random access, without manual reallocation at every push.
mechanism: Holds a backing buffer with capacity ≥ length; on overflow it allocates a larger buffer (typically 1.5–2×) and copies elements, amortising growth cost.
benefits: Excellent spatial locality and cache behaviour
Amortised O(1) push_back; O(1) indexed access
Lowest constant factors of any sequence
limits: Growth can spike latency and double peak memory transiently
failures: Unbounded growth → OOM if capacity never reserved
Holding a reference across a push that reallocates → dangling
whenNot: Hard real-time paths where a single resize spike is unacceptable
myths: "Append is always O(1)" — it is amortised, individual appends can be O(n)
